hook: Korea's premier shamanist shrine, exiled up a mountain by the Japanese
facts: First raised in 1395 to honour the mountain spirit Mongmyeok
Japanese authorities moved it to Inwangsan in 1925 to clear ground for their own Shinto shrine
Holds 21 shaman paintings; designated National Folklore Cultural Heritage No. 28
Female mudang still perform gut rituals here, just below Seonbawi rock
legend: They say the shrine houses the spirit of King Taejo, founder of Joseon
Seonbawi rock above is said to resemble a monk frozen in meditation
w: Drums, knives and pig heads still appear on the floor when a mudang summons the spirits here, on a ridge of Inwangsan. Built in 1395, the shrine got shoved up the mountain in 1925 so the Japanese could plant their own Shinto temple where it stood. They say King Taejo's spirit lives inside. Shamanism, evicted but undefeated.

hook: Grave of a queen her stepson dug up and threw out of the city
facts: Burial of Queen Sindeok, second wife of King Taejo and Joseon's first queen consort
King Taejong, her stepson, evicted the tomb beyond the city walls in 1409
Her status was demoted to mere concubine and struck from the Jongmyo register
Only restored to full royal rank in 1669, 260 years later
legend: Locals say her resentful spirit cursed the dynasty after the eviction
w: Queen Sindeok died grand and ended up disgraced. Her stepson Taejong, who'd murdered her sons for the throne, dug up her tomb in 1409, dragged it past the city walls, and demoted her to concubine. Stone from her grave got recycled into a bridge people trod on. Royal rank only came back in 1669. Spite, set in stone.

hook: Seoul's oldest restaurant, boiling ox bones for over a century
facts: Founded around 1904, in the late Korean Empire era
Survived both the Japanese colonial period and the Korean War
Broth still simmers 16 to 17 hours by the original method
Designated a Seoul Future Heritage in 2013; carries a Michelin Bib Gourmand
w: Marathon champion Sohn Kee-chung slurped here; so did a vice president and a self-styled political gangster. Open since around 1904, this milky ox-bone soup joint outlasted colonisation and war without changing the recipe. Bones boil 16 hours before a bowl reaches you. A century of seolleongtang, same pot, same patience.

hook: Red-brick house where an American reporter hid Korea's independence declaration
facts: Built 1923-24 by AP correspondent Albert W. Taylor, who named it after a palace in India
Taylor smuggled news of the 1919 March 1st Movement out to the world
A copy of the Declaration of Independence was hidden in his wife's hospital bed
Built in rare French rat-trap bond brickwork; restored as a museum in 2020
w: A gold-mine manager turned wire reporter built this red-brick pile in 1923 and named it Dilkusha, 'heart's delight', after a ruined Indian palace. Albert Taylor smuggled word of Korea's 1919 uprising to the world; a copy of the independence declaration lay hidden in his wife's bed. Japan expelled them in 1942. Delight, then exile.

hook: A 1,700-year-old Baekje rampart hiding inside Olympic Park
facts: Earthen fortress raised after the 4th century under King Geunchogo of Baekje
Part of the early Baekje capital region around the Han River
Excavations turned up stone swords, hearths and Baekje earthenware
Now grassed-over hills inside Seoul's 1988 Olympic Park
w: Joggers circle grassy mounds in Olympic Park with no idea they're rounding a Baekje war wall thrown up after the 4th century, back when Seoul served as the kingdom's capital. Dig in and out come stone swords, hearths and shards of grey pottery. The 1988 Games landscaped right over it. Ancient ramparts mistaken for a jogging hill.

hook: Burned-out remains of Japan's grandest colonial Shinto shrine
facts: Chosen Shrine stood here 1925-1945, dedicated to Amaterasu and Emperor Meiji
Koreans were forced to bow before it; Christians especially suffered
Japan burned and dismantled it themselves in 1945 on defeat
An Jung-geun Memorial Hall, honouring Ito Hirobumi's assassin, now occupies the site
w: Hundreds of steps once climbed Namsan to Japan's mightiest overseas Shinto shrine, where Koreans were marched up to bow before Amaterasu. On losing the war in 1945, the Japanese torched it themselves. Concrete footings surfaced during a 2006 dig. Today a hall honours An Jung-geun, who shot the colonial overlord. Poetic ground.

hook: Imperial sky-altar pavilion marooned behind a luxury hotel
facts: Octagonal pavilion built 1899 where Emperor Gojong worshipped Heaven
Marked the founding of the short-lived Korean Empire in 1897
Japan demolished most of the complex in 1913 to build a railway hotel
Only the pavilion, stone drums and a triple gate survive, hemmed by high-rises
w: In 1897 Korea declared itself an empire, and Emperor Gojong raised this three-tier octagonal pavilion to make sacrifices to Heaven, sovereign at last. Sixteen years on, the Japanese flattened most of it for a railway hotel. Pavilion and dragon-carved stone drums now hide in a hotel courtyard, walled in by towers. An empire's last gasp.

hook: A Buddhist temple that began as Seoul's grandest secret restaurant
facts: Site was Daewongak, a high-end gisaeng house for closed-door politics into the 1970s
Owner Kim Yeong-han, a gisaeng from age 16, donated the whole estate
Monk Beopjeong, author of 'Non-Possession', refused for years before accepting in 1995
Opened as a temple in 1997 on the wooded slope of Samgaksan
w: Politicians once cut deals over wine and gisaeng in these hillside pavilions, when this was Daewongak, one of Seoul's top three pleasure houses. Its owner, a courtesan since sixteen, gave the whole estate to a monk who preached owning nothing. He turned her down for a decade, then relented in 1995. From scandal to silence.

hook: The plain grave of Joseon's most notorious deposed tyrant
facts: Burial of Yeonsangun, the 10th Joseon king, deposed for tyranny in 1506
Stripped of royal status, so his mound carries no royal tomb honours
Moved here in 1513 at the request of his widow, Lady Sin
Quietly opened to the public only in 2006
w: Joseon's textbook tyrant lies under a modest grass mound in suburban Dobong, denied the stone guardians and grand spirit road of a proper royal tomb. Deposed in 1506 for cruelty and madness, Yeonsangun rates only the rank of 'prince'. His widow had him moved here in 1513. A king downgraded even in death.

hook: One hardware dealer's lifelong obsession with old Korean locks
facts: Opened in 2003 by former hardware-shop owner Choi Hong-gyu
Holds over 4,000 locks, just a fraction of his 5,000-strong collection
Choi spent his entire fortune to build it
Sits at the top of the Naksan fortress-wall trail
w: A Gangnam hardware dealer spent thirty years and his whole fortune amassing locks, then built a rust-coloured museum to show them off. Inside wait over 4,000 Korean padlocks, fish-shaped and turtle-shaped, their keyholes hidden by sliding plates and false fronts. Old Korea secured its valuables with riddles. Beauty in keeping things shut.

hook: Riverside sandbank where Korea's Catholic priests were beheaded
facts: Execution ground used in the persecutions of 1801, 1839, 1846 and 1866
Eleven priests died here, including Korea's first, Andrew Kim Taegon
Chinese priest Zhou Wen-mo was beheaded here in 1801
A hanok-style memorial church was consecrated on the site in 1987
w: On a sandy flat between the old city wall and the Han, the Joseon state drilled soldiers and beheaded traitors, then turned the blades on Catholic priests. Eleven died here, among them Andrew Kim Taegon, Korea's first native priest, executed in 1846 at twenty-five. A church now stands where they knelt.

hook: A 1940s market where brass coins buy your own pick-and-mix meal
facts: Market opened in 1941 for Japanese residents, then taken over by Korean vendors after the war
Around 75 stalls line the Seochon alley west of Gyeongbokgung
A 2012 cafe lets you swap cash for replica Joseon-era yeopjeon coins
Coins buy small portions stall to stall, filling your own tray
w: Hand over 5,000 won and you get a fistful of brass yeopjeon, the square-holed coins Joseon spent, then wander a 1941 market trading them for fried things and side dishes stall by stall until your tray fills. The alley fed Japanese settlers first, Koreans after the war. Pay in fake old money, eat like a magpie.
